stopReason: 2 critical finding(s) have no auto-fix (E_author_sameas_missing, E_human_signals_bundle_incomplete) — human edit required

instructions.evidence: Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
FAQPage schema has 8 Questions but page shows ~15 FAQ pairs
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Human voice vs committee 3/10 — Reads like a content template with brief injected 'spicy' lines that feel bolted on.

patch.target: In 2026, AEO centers on aligning your content with the data structures and evidence types that LLMs interpret as trustworthy.
patch.before: In 2026, AEO centers on aligning your content with the data structures and evidence types that LLMs interpret as trustworthy.
patch.after: In 2026, AEO is basically this: give the LLM a sentence it can steal without getting sued, and make the steal easy.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Human voice vs committee" scored 3/10: Reads like a content template with brief injected 'spicy' lines that feel bolted on.

instructions.evidence: Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with a textbook definition repeated three times (TL;DR, quick answer, intro) before saying anything interesting.

patch.target: Answer engine optimization (AEO) in 2026 is the discipline of making content discoverable, citable, and preferred in AI-generated answers across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
patch.before: Answer engine optimization (AEO) in 2026 is the discipline of making content discoverable, citable, and preferred in AI-generated answers across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
patch.after: Your next customer will read one paragraph about you—and a chatbot will write it. AEO is how you ghostwrite that paragraph.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Intro earns attention" scored 3/10: Opens with a textbook definition repeated three times (TL;DR, quick answer, intro) before saying anything interesting.

instructions.evidence: Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A few contrarian lines exist (schema overrated, E-E-A-T red herring) but most claims are safe and generic.

patch.target: Effective AEO focuses on intent fulfillment, authoritative evidence, and structured metadata to win mention and citation in generative search results.
patch.before: Effective AEO focuses on intent fulfillment, authoritative evidence, and structured metadata to win mention and citation in generative search results.
patch.after: Most AEO advice is noise; in our tests only three things moved citations: a quotable stat, a clean definition, and being mentioned on Reddit.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Genuine opinions vs safe claims" scored 5/10: A few contrarian lines exist (schema overrated, E-E-A-T red herring) but most claims are safe and generic.

instructions.evidence: Unexpected phrasings 4/10 — One or two vivid lines ('lazy LLM will paste', 'feels safe plagiarizing') but drowned in boilerplate phrasing.

patch.target: AEO measurement requires tracking where, how, and when your brand is cited across leading AI answer engines.
patch.before: AEO measurement requires tracking where, how, and when your brand is cited across leading AI answer engines.
patch.after: AEO measurement means stalking your own brand across four chatbots and screenshotting every time it shows up or doesn't.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Unexpected phrasings" scored 4/10: One or two vivid lines ('lazy LLM will paste', 'feels safe plagiarizing') but drowned in boilerplate phrasing.

instructions.evidence: Point of view vs both-sides survey 5/10 — Has glimmers of a POV but quickly retreats to balanced, hedged recommendations.

patch.target: To win in answer engine optimization, brands should combine people-first, authoritative content with embedded AEO signals.
patch.before: To win in answer engine optimization, brands should combine people-first, authoritative content with embedded AEO signals.
patch.after: Skip 'people-first' platitudes—pick one definition per page, back it with one primary stat, and ignore the rest of the schema zoo.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Point of view vs both-sides survey" scored 5/10: Has glimmers of a POV but quickly retreats to balanced, hedged recommendations.

instructions.evidence: Quotability 4/10 — The 'lazy LLM paste' line is quotable; most others are forgettable definitional boilerplate.

patch.target: AEO is about being cited by AI answer engines, not just ranked by search engines.
patch.before: AEO is about being cited by AI answer engines, not just ranked by search engines.
patch.after: AEO is SEO for machines that don't click—you win by being the sentence, not the link.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Quotability" scored 4/10: The 'lazy LLM paste' line is quotable; most others are forgettable definitional boilerplate.

instructions.evidence: intro_hook 6/10 — The post opens with a flat definition sentence that restates the title verbatim. There is no tension, surprising fact, or concrete scenario to earn continued reading. The TL;DR and Quick Answer blocks above it also dilute whatever hook effect remains. The first body sentence reads as throat-clearing dressed in jargon.

patch.target: Answer engine optimization (AEO) in 2026 is the discipline of making content discoverable, citable, and preferred in AI-generated answers across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
patch.before: Answer engine optimization (AEO) in 2026 is the discipline of making content discoverable, citable, and preferred in AI-generated answers across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
patch.after: A Perplexity user typed your brand's core question last week—and your competitor got cited, not you. That gap has a name: answer engine optimization.
patch.rationale: Quality axis "intro_hook" scored 6/10: The post opens with a flat definition sentence that restates the title verbatim. There is no tension, surprising fact, or concrete scenario to earn continued reading. The TL;DR and Quick Answer blocks above it also dilute whatever hook effect remains. The first body sentence reads as throat-clearing dressed in jargon.

instructions.evidence: specificity 6/10 — The measurement section collapses into abstractions at key moments—'tools that aggregate,' 'refine content to strengthen AI-evidence signals,' and 'adapt to how LLMs process, trust, and quote sources' are all vague. The Princeton GEO statistic (40% visibility boost) is a bright spot, but it appears once. The bold claim that 'schema markup is overrated' and 'a single well-cited statistic outperformed a full DefinedTerm rollout' is never anchored with sample size, test methodology, or dates, making it feel asserted rather than evidenced.

patch.target: Tools that aggregate these metrics save time and inform ongoing AEO content iterations.
patch.before: Tools that aggregate these metrics save time and inform ongoing AEO content iterations.
patch.after: Tools like Profound, Otterly.ai, and BrandMentions now track citation-share across ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity, letting you see week-over-week shifts in how often your brand appears in a given answer category.
patch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: The measurement section collapses into abstractions at key moments—'tools that aggregate,' 'refine content to strengthen AI-evidence signals,' and 'adapt to how LLMs process, trust, and quote sources' are all vague. The Princeton GEO statistic (40% visibility boost) is a bright spot, but it appears once. The bold claim that 'schema markup is overrated' and 'a single well-cited statistic outperformed a full DefinedTerm rollout' is never anchored with sample size, test methodology, or dates, making it feel asserted rather than evidenced.

priorIssues.evidence: Organization missing recommended: logo, sameAs, contactPoint
WebPage missing recommended: dateModified, isPartOf, primaryImageOfPage, inLanguage
FAQPage schema has 8 Questions but page shows ~15 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
definitional posts should have DefinedTerm schema — not present
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
17 em-dashes (4.71 per 400 words; target <1)
20 tricolons (6.92/500 words; target ≤2)
Human voice vs committee 3/10 — Reads like a content template with brief injected 'spicy' lines that feel bolted on.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A few contrarian lines exist (schema overrated, E-E-A-T red herring) but most claims are safe and generic.
Unexpected phrasing 1/10 — Phrasing is entirely predictable AI boilerplate: 'discipline of', 'intent fulfillment', 'citation-worthy signals'.
Specific citations (names/places/prices/dates) 3/10 — Names engines and vague references but no actual numbers, studies, people, prices, or dated examples.
Point of view vs both-sides survey 5/10 — Has glimmers of a POV but quickly retreats to balanced, hedged recommendations.
Quotability 4/10 — The 'lazy LLM paste' line is quotable; most others are forgettable definitional boilerplate.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with a textbook definition repeated three times (TL;DR, quick answer, intro) before saying anything interesting.

specificity 6/10 — The measurement section collapses into abstractions at key moments—'tools that aggregate,' 'refine content to strengthen AI-evidence signals,' and 'adapt to how LLMs process, trust, and quote sources' are all vague. The Princeton GEO statistic (40% visibility boost) is a bright spot, but it appears once. The bold claim that 'schema markup is overrated' and 'a single well-cited statistic outperformed a full DefinedTerm rollout' is never anchored with sample size, test methodology, or dates, making it feel asserted rather than evidenced.
